title: SIGTERM Connection Drain and Graceful Shutdown Playbook

text:
Purpose
-------
This playbook describes the operational procedure, runtime integrations, and verification steps for performing graceful shutdowns of inference runtime workers in response to SIGTERM (or equivalent orchestration signals). It covers: connection draining, inflight request completion, kernel-scheduler handoff, KV cache persistence, observability checks, and rollback. The goal is to avoid user-facing 5xx/timeout spikes and to preserve SLOs for p99 latency and error rate during deployments and preemptions.

Scope
-----
- Applies to: runtime worker processes (redwood-runtime-worker), shard proxies (kv-shard-proxy), and the request-router service.
- Deployment modes: Hosted API, Dedicated nodes, and Private single-tenant installs (behavior differs slightly; see "Mode differences" below).

Ownership and Contacts
---------------------
- Runbook owner: Runtime Operations (runtime...

text:
Overview:

This document defines a benchmark specification and playbook for measuring cost provenance and redispatch overhead across the Redwood serving runtime. The goal is to produce reproducible, SLO-driven measurements that connect observed token-level cost and latency back to routing/redispatch decisions, kernel scheduling characteristics, and model-variant selection. Results are used to validate cost-accounting telemetry, guide scheduler tuning, and certify fallback/redispatch policies before rollout.

Audience:
- Runtime engineers (kernel/scheduler)
- Performance/benchmarking team
- SRE and capacity planning
- Product owners for routing and pricing

Goals and success criteria:
- Accurate attribution: >=95% of per-request cost is attributed to a provenance chain (routing -> model-variant -> kernel profile -> quantization profile).
- Redispatch overhead measurement: measure media...

text:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is ...
